steps.title: Isolate $b^{2}$
steps.content: Start with the given equation: $b^2 + 7c = 4d$. Subtract $7c$ from both sides to isolate $b^2$: $b^2 = 4d - 7c$.

steps.title: Use the constraint
steps.content: Check that the radicand is positive. The constraint $d \gt \frac{7}{4}c$ means $4d \gt 7c$, so $4d - 7c \gt 0$. A real square root exists.

steps.title: Take both square roots
steps.content: Solve for $b$ by taking the square root of both sides. Because $b$ is a real number (not specified positive), both signs are valid: $b = \pm\sqrt{4d - 7c}$.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content:
Evaluate each choice.
  • Choice A: $b=\frac{4d-7c}{2}$ — divides by 2 instead of taking a square root. This does not come from $b^2 = 4d - 7c$. Eliminate A.
  • Choice B: $b=\frac{4d+7c}{2}$ — adds $7c$ instead of subtracting, and divides by 2. Neither step is algebraically valid here. Eliminate B.
  • Choice C: $b=\pm\sqrt{4d-7c}$ — correct: isolate $b^2$ by subtracting $7c$, then take the square root with both signs.
  • Choice D: $b=\pm\sqrt{4d+7c}$ — uses $+7c$ under the radical, which would come from adding $7c$ to both sides instead of subtracting. Eliminate D.
That matches Choice C — $b=\pm\sqrt{4d-7c}$.
